old_claim.note: Motorized Logistics supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim_absent: No direct Early Warehouse Management to Motorized Logistics edge remains because the scoped early warehouse node is anchored to mid-nineteenth-century grain elevator receipts, scale tickets, customer accounts, daily position records, grade standards, and rail-linked storage before motorized truck logistics.

source_shape.source_locator: Santos says that before the mid-1860s commission agents obtained warehouse receipts for lots and quantities of grain in store, and that by the 1840s grain elevators and railroads facilitated high-volume grain storage and shipment.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source anchors the early warehouse-management mechanism to receipts, grain elevators, railroads, and fungible inventory grades rather than motorized logistics.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A warehouse-control practice operating through rail-linked grain elevators and receipt finance before the mid-1860s cannot depend on later motorized logistics as a prerequisite.

ontology_before: Early Warehouse Management depended on Motorized Logistics, making a nineteenth-century warehouse-recordkeeping node depend on a later truck-and-tractor logistics abstraction.
ontology_after: Early Warehouse Management is scoped to rail-linked grain elevator inventory records and receipts; motorized logistics remains later freight-distribution infrastructure rather than an upstream requirement.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: removed the direct motorized_logistics prerequisite. Preserved: the node still depends on railroads for high-volume rail-linked storage and shipment.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to twentieth-century truck-depot warehouse operations.
A source shows the specific early warehouse-management practices require motor trucks or tractors rather than rail-linked elevators and receipt accounting.
A separate motorized_warehouse_logistics node is introduced for twentieth-century depot operations.
short_reason: Early warehouse receipts and grain-elevator records predate motorized logistics.
